speaker: NICO
text: Okay so Hanson's group just teleported a CNOT gate between two diamond nodes. Unconditionally. No post-selection.

speaker: VENA
text: Wait, unconditionally? With NV centers? That's a strong claim. What's the gate fidelity?

speaker: NICO
text: So they're hitting around sixty-three percent for the entangled state they generate with the gate. The classical truth table averages above seventy percent. But here's the thing—they accept every single measurement outcome and do real-time feedforward.

speaker: VENA
text: Sixty-three percent is not amazing. What's eating the fidelity?

speaker: NICO
text: Mostly the remote entanglement generation. They're getting seventy-seven percent fidelity on the Bell state between the communication qubits—those are the NV electron spins—and then local gate errors and readout errors on top of that. The actual CNOT is between carbon-13 nuclear spins, one at each node.

speaker: VENA
text: Okay so two-qubit registers per node. Electron for communication, nucleus for data. That's the standard NV playbook. How are they controlling the nuclei?

speaker: NICO
text: This is where it gets interesting. They use different methods at the two nodes. Alice is at high field, one hundred eighty-nine millitesla, and they're doing this DDRF thing—dynamical decoupling with RF pulses directly on the nuclear spin. Bob is at thirty-one millitesla, standard DD gates where you manipulate the nucleus through the electron dynamics.

speaker: VENA
text: High field on an NV? That's unusual. Are they having charge state problems?

speaker: NICO
text: They handle it with DC Stark tuning plus aggressive charge repumping at five seventy-five nanometers. The repump pulse is strongly power-broadened, like four hundred nanowatts for Alice, and it keeps the NV minus state stable enough to do entanglement generation. The Stark tuning gets both nodes' optical transitions on resonance at four seventy point four five five terahertz.

speaker: VENA
text: And the entanglement generation—how fast is it? Because if you're doing unconditional gates you need the data qubits to survive however long it takes to herald entanglement.

speaker: NICO
text: Right. So single entanglement attempt is eight point four microseconds. They're using photonic number-state encoding, unbalanced superposition, emit a photon, interfere at a beam splitter. Success probability is about one minus alpha where alpha is around point zero four to point zero six. They set a timeout at fifty attempts before reinitializing.

speaker: VENA
text: Fifty attempts is four hundred-ish microseconds. What's the nuclear coherence under network activity?

speaker: NICO
text: They characterize it as N one over e, the number of attempts where fidelity drops by one over e. Alice gets three hundred ninety-one attempts, Bob gets four seventy-nine. So the timeout at fifty attempts is conservative but it's a trade-off—longer timeout means the data qubits dephase more if entanglement takes a while.

speaker: VENA
text: Those coherence numbers are way better than fifty. Why not push the timeout higher?

speaker: NICO
text: Practical reasons. The nuclear spin picks up stochastic phase during each attempt because the electron reset timing is probabilistic. They do real-time phase tracking—for Alice it's updating the RF local oscillator phase, for Bob it's compiled lookup tables of DD sequences. But longer timeouts mean more accumulated error and lower overall fidelity. At fifty attempts the state fidelity is still reasonable.

speaker: VENA
text: Okay but let's talk about the gate itself. How does the teleportation protocol actually work here?

speaker: NICO
text: Standard gate teleportation. Generate a Bell state between the communication qubits. Do local gates to entangle each communication qubit with its local data qubit. Then mid-circuit readout of both communication qubits, and the outcomes tell you what Pauli corrections to apply to the target data qubit. The correction is done in real-time with feedforward.

speaker: VENA
text: And the readout fidelity on the electrons?

speaker: NICO
text: Single-shot, spin-selective optical readout. They quote around five percent wrong assignment on a measured one outcome for Alice, nine percent for Bob. That directly kills the gate fidelity because wrong readout means wrong feedforward operation.

speaker: VENA
text: Nine percent is rough. So if I measure one but it's actually zero, I apply the wrong correction and the gate fails. They simulate this?

speaker: NICO
text: Yeah. They ran a sim where they only accept zero-zero mid-circuit outcomes, no feedforward needed. In that post-selected case the truth table fidelities go to ninety percent and the entangled state hits seventy-six percent. So the readout is definitely the next thing to improve.

speaker: VENA
text: Seventy-six versus sixty-three is a big gap. That's annoying because readout is a solved problem in principle—you just need better SNR or longer integration.

speaker: NICO
text: Exactly. But they're also doing non-destructive readout on the communication qubit during tomography so the data qubit doesn't dephase. It's all about those weak laser pulses, dynamical stop when you see a photon. Trade-offs everywhere.

speaker: VENA
text: Fine. Let's talk about the four-qubit GHZ state because that's actually a cleaner benchmark than the gate, right?

speaker: NICO
text: Yeah, same primitives but no feedforward on the data qubits, only on the communication qubits to correct the Bell state phase. They get sixty-four percent fidelity, and that exceeds the fifty percent threshold for genuine four-partite entanglement. Fully heralded, no post-selection.

speaker: VENA
text: Sixty-four percent. What's the simulated value?

speaker: NICO
text: Sixty-six percent. So they're pretty much on the money. The limiting factors are the remote entangled state at seventy-seven percent, data qubit initialization around eighty-five and ninety-six percent for Alice and Bob, plus the tomography errors from communication qubit readout.

speaker: VENA
text: Okay so this is a system demonstration, not a high-fidelity gate. The point is that it works without post-selection and the classical communication actually closes the loop in real time.

speaker: NICO
text: Right. And they're at two meters of fiber but the architecture is extensible. Same group already did ten kilometers with deployed fiber for entanglement generation. The gate protocol doesn't care about distance once you have the entangled state.

speaker: VENA
text: Sure, but at ten kilometers your entanglement rate tanks and your data qubits better have crazy long coherence. Are they claiming this scales?

speaker: NICO
text: They're not claiming it's ready to scale right now. But they point out you could go fully deterministic—no timeout—if you get the active link efficiency above one. That means data qubit coherence under network activity has to exceed the average time to generate entanglement. They cite recent work with weakly coupled carbons getting way longer coherence, like orders of magnitude better.

speaker: VENA
text: So use a different nuclear spin. What about their phase tracking—does that generalize?

speaker: NICO
text: Yeah that's actually one of the key methods here. The DDRF phase tracking is simpler at high field because the nuclear Larmor frequency is higher and you're directly driving it. For a weakly coupled nucleus you'd still be in that regime. For DD-based control like Bob's setup it's more complex but they built the lookup table infrastructure.

speaker: VENA
text: What's the experimental rate for the full gate sequence?

speaker: NICO
text: Twenty-three to forty-two millihertz depending on daily fluctuations in photon counts and charge stability. They ran one-hour batches with recalibration in between. Total data points: three sixty for GHZ, four hundred for the truth table, two thirty-four for the entangled state via CNOT.

speaker: VENA
text: Millihertz. So you're sitting there for hours collecting a few hundred points.

speaker: NICO
text: Welcome to NV networking. But honestly that's not terrible for a four-qubit protocol with two cryostats and real-time control. The rate is limited by entanglement generation, which is limited by photon emission probability and collection efficiency.

speaker: VENA
text: And they mention cavity enhancement as a path forward. That would help the rate but cavities add their own complications—coupling, frequency matching, all that.

speaker: NICO
text: True. Or you switch to a different emitter entirely. They mention group-four color centers, SiV, GeV, those have better optical properties. The DDRF and phase tracking methods they developed here could transfer over.

speaker: VENA
text: Okay so bottom line: they've demonstrated the primitives you need for distributed quantum computing on solid-state qubits. Heralded entanglement, local multi-qubit control, real-time feedforward, no post-selection. Fidelities are modest but the architecture works.

speaker: NICO
text: Exactly. And the four-qubit GHZ is the largest heralded entangled state across optically connected solid-state nodes. Previous NV network stuff was either post-selected or didn't have the full gate teleportation with feedforward.

speaker: VENA
text: What's the honest limitation they admit to?

speaker: NICO
text: Readout fidelity on the communication qubits is the big one. Also they acknowledge the timeout strategy means it's not fully deterministic yet—if you don't get entanglement in fifty attempts you abort and reinitialize. To go fully deterministic you need better data qubit coherence or much faster entanglement generation or both.

speaker: VENA
text: And presumably once you fix those things you can start doing actual distributed algorithms, error correction, that kind of thing.

speaker: NICO
text: Yeah they specifically call out distributed error correction as a next step. The DDRF multi-qubit control would let you encode on several nuclei per node, then the gate teleportation lets you do non-local stabilizers. It's a testbed for that now.

speaker: VENA
text: Alright. I'm convinced it's real. Not blown away by the fidelities but the no-post-selection claim holds up and the methods are solid.

speaker: NICO
text: And honestly for NV centers this is a pretty big step. High magnetic field, DDRF control, Stark tuning, real-time phase tracking during entanglement—none of that was integrated before. It's a lot of engineering.

speaker: VENA
text: Fair. I'd want to see this with better readout and then we can talk about real applications. But it's a good proof of concept.
